content: Vestibular disorders cause vertigo (illusion of motion), dizziness, imbalance. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is most common cause of vertigo—brief (<1 minute) episodes triggered by head position changes (rolling in bed, looking up), caused by otoconia dislodged into semicircular canals (posterior canal most common). Vestibular neuritis: acute prolonged vertigo (days-weeks) from viral inflammation of vestibular nerve, no hearing loss. Labyrinthitis: vestibular neuritis plus hearing loss (cochlear involvement). Ménière's disease: episodic vertigo (20 minutes to 12 hours), fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness; caused by endolymphatic hydrops (increased endolymph pressure). Vestibular migraine: vertigo episodes with migraine features or history. Bilateral vestibulopathy: oscillopsia (visual blurring with head movement), imbalance from bilateral vestibular loss (ototoxicity like gentamicin, autoimmune, degenerative). Central causes of vertigo: stroke (cerebellar, brainstem), multiple sclerosis, tumors (acoustic neuroma/vestibular schwannoma).
